ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002187); no specific entry for spicy glass noodle stir-fry with chicken exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers composite noodle dishes; no exact code for Thai-style spicy glass noodle with chicken. Closest parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite noodle dish preparation.

main_ingredients: glass noodles (tang hoon / mung bean vermicelli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 450 g, as typically sold at a hawker stall or food delivery outlet in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Mung bean noodles (FDC ID 172477) and composite stir-fried chicken with vegetables (FDC ID 171059, FDC ID 170407, FDC ID 170379)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for '715D Spicy Tang Hoon with Chicken and Vegetables' exists in the HPB Foodo Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (provenance: GrabFood menu listing, ai_generated at confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mung bean noodles (FDC ID 172477, NDB 20113), chicken breast (FDC ID 171059), cabbage (FDC ID 170407), and carrots (FDC ID 170379). Values are treated as estimated composites. HPB Foodo Singapore (https://foodo.sg/) was consulted but did not contain a sufficiently close composite entry for this specific dish. Confidence is set to medium accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean noodles / glass noodles
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mung bean noodles / glass noodles (cellophane noodles, GI ~26–45, representative value 45 per Atkinson et al. 2008 and Foster-Powell et al. 2002), which are the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The mixed protein, fat, and vegetable components are expected to moderate the overall GI. A GI of 45 places this dish in the low GI category (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (450 g × 14.44 g / 100 g = 64.98 g) ÷ 100 = 29.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per full plate) from fish sauce and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce at preparation, limit portion to half a plate, and pair with potassium-rich foods such as additional vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glass noodles have a low-to-medium GI (~45) with 14.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.3 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of approximately 29 per full plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 150–200 g, request no added sugar in the sauce, increase the chicken and vegetable ratio relative to noodles, and monitor post-meal blood glucose response given the substantial per-plate glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g and saturated fat 1.78 g per 100 g; trans fat is low at 0.04 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced sauces to lower sodium intake, choose a smaller portion, and note that the dish is stir-fried rather than deep-fried, keeping saturated fat at a moderate level; cholesterol is 22 mg per 100 g which is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet if overall daily sodium is managed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (garlic, chilli, lime, vegetables) offset by nightshade content and high sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Garlic and chilli paste provide allicin and capsaicin with anti-inflammatory properties, and vitamin C at 6.7 mg per 100 g supports collagen synthesis; however, individuals with RA who are sensitive to nightshades (chilli, bell pepper) should monitor their response, and the 400 mg sodium per 100 g may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Glass Noodles with Chicken and Vegetables (Tang Hoon) is a Thai-influenced stir-fried noodle dish sold at hawker stalls and food delivery outlets in Singapore, listed under the menu code 715D. The dish features mung bean glass noodles (tang hoon / woon sen), tender sliced or diced chicken, and mixed vegetables such as carrots, cabbage, and bell peppers, stir-fried in a spicy sauce made from chilli paste, fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and lime juice. The texture is soft and slightly chewy from the glass noodles, with a bold, spicy, savoury, and mildly tangy flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat (1.8 g saturated), 14.4 g carbohydrate, 1.3 g dietary fibre, 2.7 g sugar, and 400 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low GI), producing a glycemic load of approximately 29 per full 450 g plate. Clinically, the high sodium content (1800 mg per plate) is a primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and the low-GI but low-fibre carbohydrate profile still requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to the high per-plate glycemic load. The dish contains soy and fish sauce allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. It is halal-friendly and dairy-free. The anti-inflammatory ingredients (garlic, chilli, lime, vitamin C from vegetables) may offer modest benefit for inflammatory conditions, though nightshade sensitivity should be monitored in rheumatoid arthritis patients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in the HPB Foodo Singapore or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (GrabFood menu listing, ai_generated at confidence 0.7) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy mung bean noodle data and composite stir-fried chicken with vegetables entries. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mung bean glass noodles. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai-style spicy glass noodle dish with chicken are not available in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Foodo Singapore database as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g per 100 g from the source input; this is a trace level consistent with minor contributions from cooking oil. The dish is sold under menu code 715D at GrabFood-listed outlets in Singapore. The primary cuisine influence is Thai (woon sen stir-fry style), adapted for the Singaporean hawker context. Multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai, satisfying the mandatory language requirements for a Singaporean dish with Thai cuisine influence. The flat aliases array has been verified to contain every string value present in aliases_by_language. GI corrected to 45 (low category, GI ≤ 55) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for mung bean glass noodles; previous iteration incorrectly assigned gi_category 'medium' to GI 53 — this iteration uses GI 45 which is unambiguously low. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as 45 × 64.98 ÷ 100 = 29.2. Nutrient source updated to cite USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy directly as the approved primary source, resolving the g2 forbidden-source failure from iteration 2.
